content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 3, 2001

Daniel J. McHugh, City Attorney
Office of the City Attorney
City of Redlands
Post Office Box 3005
Redlands, CA 92373-1505

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-01-219

Dear Mr. McHugh:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is limited solely to provisions of the Act, and should not be taken as advice or an opinion regarding any other areas of law potentially raised by your letter. This advice is based upon the facts as provided in your request letter and subsequent telephone calls.² Please note that this letter should not be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. (Reg. 18329, subd.(b)(8)(A).)

QUESTION

May City of Redlands Councilmember John Freedman participate in decisions of the city council concerning a lawsuit filed by the Redlands Association against the city?

_______________

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

² This advice is applicable and confers immunity only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct, and that all of the material facts have been disclosed. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71; § 83114.)

File No. A-01-219
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

Councilmember Freedman may participate in decisions of the city council
concerning a lawsuit filed by the Redlands Association against the city because he has no
economic interest that is involved in the lawsuit.

FACTS

The Redlands Association is a non-profit corporation which acts as a "citizens
watchdog group" relating to matters affecting the city and the citizens of Redlands.
Councilmember Freedman is a member of the Redlands Association. The Redlands
Association has filed a lawsuit against the city challenging the city's approval of a
settlement agreement with the County of San Bernardino and a private party. The
settlement agreement relates to development in an unincorporated area of the county that
is not within the city's sphere of influence.

Councilmember Freedman knows of no financial interest that he might have in the
Redlands Association itself, the lawsuit, or the settlement agreement that is being
challenged by the lawsuit. The members of the Redlands Association do not receive any
financial benefit as a result of their membership in the non-profit corporation. In
subsequent telephone conversations with you, information was provided to the effect that
Councilmember Freedman's dues to the Redlands Association will not be affected by the
outcome of the suit and that, as a member of the association, he is not responsible for any
debts it may incur. Further, he has no real property interest, business interest, or source
of income that is associated with the underlying settlement agreement, which is the
subject of the lawsuit.

ANALYSIS

The Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. (§ 87100.) Pursuant to Regulation 18700, an eight-
step analysis is applied to determine whether a public official has a conflict of interest in
a given governmental decision.

Step One: Is the individual a "public official?"

As a city council member, Mr. Freedman is a public official within the meaning
of the Act. (§ 82048.)

Step Two: Is the public official making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision?

In participating as a council member in determining what actions the city may or
may not take with respect to the lawsuit filed by the Redlands Association and/or the

File No. A-01-219
Page No. 3

underlying settlement that gave rise to that law suit, Councilmember Freedman is making
or participating in making a governmental decision. (Reg. 18702.1, subd. (a).)

Step Three: What is the "economic interest" of the public official?

In this step, the economic interest that may give rise to a potential financial
interest in a given governmental decision is examined. The various economic interests of
a public official are as follows:

Business Interests. An official has an economic interest in a business entity in which the
official, the official's spouse, or the official's dependent children or anyone acting on the
official's behalf has invested $2,000 or more, or in which the official is a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (§ 87103, subds. (a),
(d).) The Act does not include nonprofit organizations under the definition of a "business
entity."

Real Property. The official has an economic interest in real property in which the official,
the official's spouse, the official's dependent children or anyone acting on the official's
behalf has invested $2,000 or more (including leasehold interests). (§ 87103, subd. (b).)

Sources of Income. The official has an economic interest in anyone, whether an
individual or an organization, from whom the official has received (or by whom the
official has been promised) $500 or more in income within the12 months prior to the
decision. (§ 87103, subd. (c).)

Sources of Gifts. The official has an economic interest in anyone, whether an individual
or an organization, which has given the official gifts totaling $320 or more within the 12
months prior to the decision. (§ 87103, subd. (e).)

Personal Finances. The official has an economic interest in the official's personal
expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of the official's immediate
family--this is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. If these are likely to go up
or down as a result of the governmental decision, then it has a "personal financial effect"
on the official. (Reg. 18703.5)

The information that you have provided discloses that the Redlands Association,
as a nonprofit organization, does not constitute an economic interest within the meaning
of the Act. Further, aside from his involvement in the association, no other economic
interests of the council member in real property, businesses, sources of income, gifts or
personal financial effects are in any way involved in the underlying settlement which is
the subject of the Redlands Association's lawsuit against the city. As such,
Councilmember Freedman has no economic interest in the decision within the meaning of
the Act.

In an almost identical situation involving the Redlands Association, we have
previously advised that a council member who was the president of the association had

File No. A-01-219
Page No. 4

no economic interest in the association and could participate in litigation decisions
involving the association. (McHugh Advice Letter, No. A-94-347.) In a similar vein, we
have advised that absent a financial interest, the Act does not limit or restrict an official's
membership in nongovernmental advocacy groups nor involvement in governmental
decisions concerning such groups. (McKinsey Advice Letter, No. I-00-212.)

Based on the above, the Act does not prohibit or otherwise restrict
Councilmember Freedman from participating in council decisions concerning the
Redlands Association's litigation or the settlement agreement giving rise to that
litigation.³

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: William L. Williams, Jr.
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

WLW:jg
I:\AdviceLtrs\01-219

³ Because Councilmember Freedman does not have an economic interest in the decision, it is
unnecessary to further analyze your request under the remaining steps. (See Regulations 18704-18708.)

or participating in making a governmental decision (Reg 18702.1, subd. (a).)
